home.title: Francisca Miranda
home.intro:
Based in Porto, I’m a designer & developer currently pursuing a Master’s Degree at FEUP.
My work explores the intersection of art and technology through careful curation, self improvement and experimentation.

paragraphs: These selected projects documents websites made, some in collaboration with designer Eduardo Hora.
The pages are embedded so they can be explored directly inside the portfolio.

FM is my personal brand in development. This project brings together the different iterations of my personal website. It documents how I have been improving the way I present myself and my work. Each version began in Figma, where I could test hierarchy, typography, layout, and rhythm, before translating it into code.

At its core, FM reflects an interest in self-improvement and self-curation. I see my personal branding as a continuous process of intention and taste, making sure my work is presented with the same level of care that goes into creating it. The selected screens show that evolution through different approaches to navigation, editorial structure, and tone, while maintaining the same goal.

items.title: quem me viu e quem me vê
items.alt: Quem me viu e quem me vê cover

paragraphs: quem me viu e quem me vê is an original short story and editorial design project developed for an Interactive Narratives course at FEUP. The project required writing a linear narrative around the theme of cultural identity.

The design concept came from the title itself. Quem me viu e quem me vê is a Portuguese expression used when someone looks at their present life and can barely recognize the person they used to be; said with a particular mix of bewilderment, tenderness, and disbelief. The visual language needed to hold that same tension. The page borders, designed in Illustrator, are built entirely from the repeated text of the title, rendered so small it reads as decorative line work at first glance.
The blurred calligraphy functions as a visual ghost of the title, present but not fully legible, mirroring the story's central tension.

The project was assembled in Illustrator, and the display typography and cover designed in Photoshop. The typographic hierarchy, the dotted act indicators, and the table of contents framing were all considered as extensions of the narrative rather than purely decorative choices.

Quem me viu e quem me vê process sketch {n}

paragraphs: away with the Fairies is a poster developed in Adobe Illustrator from an observational starting point: a gate on the FEUP campus. Using the Rectangle Grid Tool as a generative base, the project translates that architectural reference into a more stylised visual composition.

The work explores how a real element from the built environment can be abstracted into a creative graphic system, balancing repetition, symmetry, and decorative rhythm with a more contemporary poster sensibility. The intention was to reinterpret the object original and build an image that feels both structured and atmospheric.
It is a small but formative study in observation, abstraction, and composition, and in using digital tools not only for execution, but for discovering visual possibilities within an initial idea.

Jenkins was developed for the course unit Programming III, whose proposal was to create a program in Processing that produces a sequence of generative images to create a real-time video based on the user's sound input. The goal was to create a video with a mixed methodology that explores the aesthetic and technical possibilities of Processing.

The code incorporates various inputs: the letters S, D, and H; the arrow keys; the numbers from 1 to 5; the left mouse button; and mouse dragging. By pressing keys 1 to 5, different images from the film Marie Antoinette (2006) appear, overlap, and disappear when the same keys are pressed again.

The output includes graphic animations that react to sound, such as a sound wave, triangles generated through mouse movement, circles that appear when the D key is pressed, random geometric shapes generated by clicking the H key, circles that appear with the S key and are removed by the mouse, and circles triggered by the arrow keys.

This project was done in collaboration with my colleague Alice Logvina. We worked together to produce the various components in Processing. She handled the 3D objects like the cube and the terrain, and I handled the programming of the other elements.
Later, in collaboration with the Interface Design course unit, this project evolved into an interactive installation using Processing and an Arduino simultaneously, thus creating 5 Shades of Pink.
Me and my colleague Alice Logvina once again worked together, and we both worked on all components such as coding in Processing and utilizing the Arduino.

For this project, we used the graphic developments from our midterm work and introduced interactivity through Arduino and a webcam. The webcam, detecting light, generates small triangles in the illuminated area. The cube also rotates depending on the position of the light. In addition, with the Arduino, we had 4 buttons in the center of the room to switch between these components.
afterglow was developed in the final semester of my Bachelor’s Degree as an individual project that combines game design, illustration, interface design, animation, and programming.
The project explores how a point-and-click structure can support a calm and immersive experience centered on atmosphere rather than urgency.

Set in a visually stylized world inspired by cozy gaming and contemporary visual culture, the game invites players to move through spaces at their own pace, discovering interactions through observation and curiosity. Instead of directing the player through explicit instructions, the experience encourages intuitive exploration.

This approach is reinforced by a mostly diegetic interface, where information emerges from the game world itself. By reducing external UI noise and relying on visual cues, the project prioritizes emotional continuity, detail, and a sense of presence.

Mechanically, Afterglow remains intentionally light: players navigate environments, inspect objects, and uncover hidden elements, with progression built around free-form interaction rather than task pressure. The absence of strict goals or tutorials turns replayability into a natural consequence of discovery.

Conceptually, the project also reflects on gender representation, accessibility, and the value of aesthetics in game design. It positions visual sensibility as a legitimate design language and contributes to a broader conversation about more diverse, welcoming forms of play.

As a final outcome, Afterglow became both a personal and academic milestone: a cohesive project that demonstrates technical growth while preserving a clear artistic identity across illustration, design, sound, and interaction.
